sub_questions.question: Who originally claimed the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels?
sub_questions.answer: The claim originates from a fictional scenario analysis presented as a news report from April 27, 2026, by Muflil Hidayat on discoveryalert.com.au. Within this fictional scenario, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) is described as having announced the closure.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: The 2026 Hormuz closure was triggered by military conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with hostilities escalating from late February 2026.

sub_questions.question: What specifically does 'effectively closed' mean in the context of the claim?
sub_questions.answer: In this fictional 2026 scenario, 'effectively closed' means the IRGC issued warnings, forbade passage, boarded and attacked merchant ships, and laid sea mines. It also mentions a proposed legislative bill for a permanent ban on US/Israel-linked vessels, and that traffic through the strait had fallen to near zero by the ninth week of the blockade.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) issued warnings forbidding passage through the strait, boarded and attacked merchant ships, and laid sea mines. Iran also announced that the strait was closed to any vessel going "to and from" the ports of the US, Israel, and their allies.

sub_questions.question: When was this claim originally made or reported by a primary source?
sub_questions.answer: The fictional event is described as beginning in late February 2026, with the closure announced by the IRGC within that scenario. The report about this fictional event was published on April 27, 2026, by Muflil Hidayat on discoveryalert.com.au.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: The 2026 Hormuz closure was triggered by military conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with hostilities escalating from late February 2026.

sub_questions.question: Where was this claim first reported by a reputable source, and what was the original language if applicable?
sub_questions.answer: The origin appears to be a fictional scenario described in an article on discoveryalert.com.au. The article is in English and presents a hypothetical future event as if it has already occurred. It is not a report of a real-world event.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: That moment arrived in early 2026, and the Strait of Hormuz oil supply disruption is now cascading through energy markets...

sub_questions.question: Is there any official statement or directive from a relevant authority (e.g., Iran, US, Israel, international maritime organizations) confirming or denying this closure?
sub_questions.answer: There is no official statement from any real-world authority confirming a current closure of the Strait of Hormuz to US/Israel-linked vessels. The IRGC's announcement is part of the fictional 2026 scenario, not a current event.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) issued warnings forbidding passage through the strait...
